# UC-16.6.1 · PagerDuty Escalation Policy Timeout Rate — Incidents Missing Step Acknowledgements

## Description

Escalation policies encode contractual response times; when acknowledgements consistently arrive after configured step timeouts, teams burn trust in paging and auto-escalations become noise. Measuring timeout_rate per policy surfaces rotation and training gaps.

## Value

Improves MTTA for customer-impacting services and validates on-call process investments.

## Implementation

Map `step_timeout` from exported escalation policy JSON if not on each incident. Treat `missed` carefully for low-urgency incidents. Pair with schedule coverage UCs for root cause.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure: PagerDuty REST API poller or Event Orchestration webhook normalizer.
• Data: `index=alerts` `sourcetype=pagerduty:incident` with `escalation_policy`, `escalation_levels`, `first_acknowledgement_at`, `created_at`.
• For directory layout and inputs, see docs/implementation-guide.md.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Map `step_timeout` from exported escalation policy JSON if not on each incident. Treat `missed` carefully for low-urgency incidents. Pair with schedule coverage UCs for root cause.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following search (then save as report or alert; tune time range and threshold):

```spl
index=alerts sourcetype="pagerduty:incident"
| eval created_epoch=coalesce(created_at_epoch, strptime(created_at, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"))
| eval ack_epoch=coalesce(first_acknowledgement_at_epoch, strptime(first_acknowledgement_at, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"))
| eval ack_delay_sec=ack_epoch-created_epoch
| eval step_timeout=coalesce(escalation_policy_step_timeout_sec, 600)
| eval missed_step=if(isnull(ack_epoch) OR ack_delay_sec > step_timeout,1,0)
| bin _time span=1d
| stats count as inc, sum(missed_step) as missed by _time, escalation_policy, service_name
| eval timeout_rate=round(100*missed/nullif(inc,0),2)
| where timeout_rate > 15 AND inc >= 5
| sort -timeout_rate
```

Understanding this search
Escalation policies encode contractual response times; when acknowledgements consistently arrive after configured step timeouts, teams burn trust in paging and auto-escalations become noise. Measuring timeout_rate per policy surfaces rotation and training gaps.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with the PagerDuty incident timeline and service view for the same window. Reconcile a few incident numbers, state changes, and acknowledgement times. If anything disagrees, check export lag, time zone, and which API fields we map.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add to a dashboard, wire alert actions, and document ownership. Suggested view: Line chart (timeout_rate by policy), stacked bar (missed vs met), table of worst services.

## SPL

```spl
index=alerts sourcetype="pagerduty:incident"
| eval created_epoch=coalesce(created_at_epoch, strptime(created_at, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"))
| eval ack_epoch=coalesce(first_acknowledgement_at_epoch, strptime(first_acknowledgement_at, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"))
| eval ack_delay_sec=ack_epoch-created_epoch
| eval step_timeout=coalesce(escalation_policy_step_timeout_sec, 600)
| eval missed_step=if(isnull(ack_epoch) OR ack_delay_sec > step_timeout,1,0)
| bin _time span=1d
| stats count as inc, sum(missed_step) as missed by _time, escalation_policy, service_name
| eval timeout_rate=round(100*missed/nullif(inc,0),2)
| where timeout_rate > 15 AND inc >= 5
| sort -timeout_rate
```

## Visualization

Line chart (timeout_rate by policy), stacked bar (missed vs met), table of worst services.
